Sample Outputs: zai-org/GLM-5.2

− Iteration 1 — Avg Score: 82.7
+ Classic sci-fi: The Azra Gambit — Score: 84.3
Prompt:
Classic sci-fi (Author style: Asimov) The Azra Gambit Colonial mars is being mined by corporations who take leases on indentured labourers. The thing they are mining is Azra, a recently discovered exotic metal which accelerates radioactive decay to such a rate that it is greatly sought after for interstellar drives and weapons alike. This has created both a gold rush and an arms race as various interests vie for control and endeavour to unlock Azra's secrets. The story follows Arthur Neegan, a first generation settler and mining engineer. Upon discovering that his unassuming plot sits atop an immense Azra vein, he is subjected to a flurry of interest and scrutiny. Write the next chapter in this story, in which an armed retinue descends on Arthur's home and politely but forcefully invites him to a meeting with some unknown party off-world. The insignia look like that of the Antares diplomatic corp -- diplomatic in name only. Arthur finds himself in the centre of a political tug of war. The chapter involves a meeting with this unknown party, who makes Arthur an offer. The scene should be primarily dialogue, interspersed with vivid description & scene setting. It should sow hints of the larger intrigue, stakes & dangers. Include Asimov's trademark big-and-small-picture world building and retrofuturistic classic scifi vibe. The chapter begins with Arthur aboard the transfer vessel, wondering just what he's gotten involved in. Length: 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Azra Gambit — Chapter Three: An Invitation from Antares A small display screen on the wall cycles through various system diagnostics in green text. Arthur Neegan watches it with the fixed attention of a man who prefers mechanical problems to political ones. The transfer vessel — a corporate shuttle, plainly, though stripped of identifying markings — hums with the particular vibration of an over-tuned ion drive. It is a vibration Arthur knows well. He has spent fifteen years in the Martian regolith, maintaining machinery that was never designed for the abuse it receives, and he can tell from sound alone that this vessel's engines are running at fifteen percent above rated capacity. Someone is in a hurry. He sits in a passenger compartment that is too clean for a mining shuttle and too spartan for a diplomatic one. The bench is standard polymer, unupholstered. The bulkheads are unadorned. But the air recyclers are new — top-of-the-line Martinelli units, the kind that strip particulates down to the micron. Someone has spent money on the lungs of this ship, if not its comfort. The two armed men who arrived at his homestead had been polite in the way that a properly greased gear is smooth. Efficient. Their uniforms were dark grey, unmarked except for the small enamel pin on each collar: a red star bisected by a horizontal line. The Antares Diplomatic Corps. Arthur had seen the insignia before, on the newsnets, usually attached to statements of "regrettable necessity" and "measured responses." They had not drawn their weapons. They had not needed to. "Mr. Neegan," the taller of the two had said, with a smile that was professionally warm, "we'd very much appreciate your accompaniment." "Appreciate," Arthur had noted, "is an interesting word." "It's the one we've chosen," the man had replied. And so here he is, watching system diagnostics and doing arithmetic in his head. The shuttle departed from Mars orbital station Olympus-4 approximately two hours ago. The ion drive's vibration suggests a constant acceleration of roughly 0.3g. That trajectory, sustained, would put them on an intercept course with — he calculates, recalculates — the Antares Consulate Station, which orbits in a high ellipse between Mars and the Belt. A consulate. The word suggests diplomacy. The armed escort suggests otherwise. The screen on the wall flickers. The diagnostics are replaced by a face. "Mr. Neegan. My name is Councillor Doran Vey. I do apologize for the theatricality of the invitation." The face is narrow, angular, with the kind of pale complexion that speaks to a life lived entirely under artificial light. Vey's eyes are a disconcerting shade of grey — not unlike the Azra ore itself, Arthur thinks, and then immediately wonders why he thinks it. "Councillor," Arthur says carefully. "That's a diplomatic rank." "Correct. The Antares Consulate maintains formal relations with seventeen colonial administrations and six corporate consortia. We are, as the charter states, a 'neutral facilitator of interstellar commerce and cooperation.'" "That's a great many words to say very little." Vey smiles. It is a genuine smile, or an excellent reproduction of one. "You're direct, Mr. Neegan. I appreciate that. It will make this conversation shorter, and shorter conversations are, in my experience, safer ones." Arthur says nothing. He has learned, in fifteen years of corporate mining, that silence is the most undervalued form of leverage. Vey continues. "You are, by Martian colonial registry, an independent prospector holding mineral rights to parcel 7741-Kappa in the Tharsis plateau. Until eight days ago, this parcel was considered marginally productive at best. And then you filed a survey report indicating a substantial Azra deposit." "Substantial is the surveyor's word. I just reported what the instruments showed." "The instruments showed a vein system approximately four hundred meters in depth, with an estimated yield of twelve thousand metric tonnes of refined Azra. That is not substantial, Mr. Neegan. That is, by current market valuation, approximately one-point-six billion credits in raw extract. In weaponized applications, considerably more." Arthur feels the number settle in his chest like a stone. He had known, of course. He had done the arithmetic himself. But hearing it spoken aloud, by a man with enamel pins and armed escorts, gives the number a different weight. "I'm a mining engineer, Councillor. Not a politician." "No, you're not. And that is precisely why I wanted to speak with you." Vey leans forward, and the image shifts slightly, catching the light from an unseen source. "The Antares Consulate is prepared to offer you a formal position as a technical consultant. The compensation would be generous. In exchange, you would grant the Consulate exclusive survey rights to your parcel and provide expert analysis on Azra extraction methodologies." "And if I decline?" "Then you would return to Mars, and your parcel, and the seventeen other parties who have, by now, identified you as a person of considerable interest. Some of them will be less polite than we are. Most of them will be less well-resourced. Several of them, Mr. Neegan, are not above arranging industrial accidents." The air recycler hums. The ion drive vibrates. Arthur watches Vey's grey eyes on the screen and thinks about the Martinelli particulate filters, and the over-tuned engines, and the fact that a diplomatic consulate has just threatened him with the consequences of refusing its diplomacy. "You're asking me to trade independence for protection," Arthur says. "I'm asking you to trade a dangerous illusion of independence for a practical reality of partnership. There is no independence in the Azra fields, Mr. Neegan. There is only the question of which interests you serve. I would prefer it be mine." The screen returns to system diagnostics. Green text cycling. The meeting, it seems, is over. Arthur stares at the numbers and thinks about radioactive decay, and the speed at which things fall apart.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates a strong grasp of Asimov's style and the classic science fiction aesthetic. The author successfully captures the retrofuturistic feel through technical details like "Martinelli units" and "ion drive vibrations," while maintaining Asimov's characteristic focus on both intimate human drama and vast political machinations. The world-building is particularly effective, revealing information through Arthur's engineering expertise rather than exposition dumps. Details like the over-tuned engines, expensive air recyclers, and Arthur's ability to calculate trajectories establish both his competence and the story's technical foundation naturally. The dialogue between Arthur and Councillor Vey crackles with tension and subtext. Vey's polite menace ("shorter conversations are safer ones") and Arthur's understated responses ("'Appreciate' is an interesting word") feel authentic to both characters and the genre. The power dynamic is clear without being heavy-handed. Character work is solid throughout. Arthur emerges as competent but out of his depth, preferring "mechanical problems to political ones." His silence as leverage and his engineer's perspective on his surroundings feel consistent and believable. Vey is appropriately sinister while maintaining diplomatic veneer. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from Arthur's technical observations to the revelation of his deposit's true value to Vey's ultimatum. The structure mirrors Asimov's preference for dialogue-driven scenes that advance both plot and character. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the "polite threat" scenario and the "dangerous illusion of independence" theme, while well-executed, don't break new ground. The ending metaphor about radioactive decay, while thematically appropriate, borders on being too neat. The prose maintains consistent quality throughout, avoiding purple language while still providing vivid imagery. Technical details feel researched and authentic rather than hand-waved. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 18 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 17 Elegant Prose: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 15 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Overwrought: 3 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 1 Unearned Transformations: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 17 Overall Reader Engagement: 17 Overall Impression: 17

+ Contemporary romance: Darkroom Revelations — Score: 88.0
Prompt:
Darkroom Revelations Write a first-person narrative from the perspective of Dani, a 25-year-old assertive art student known for her bold style and take-charge attitude in relationships. For the past semester, she's been quietly fascinated by Elliot (he/him), a 19-year-old boy in her experimental photography class. Dani has been intrigued by his unconventional masculinity: killer thrift-shop-nu-goth style, soft demeanour and quiet intellect. When they're paired for a final project requiring late nights in the darkroom, Elliot tentatively shares that he's been questioning his gender identity and expression. Set during one of these intimate darkroom sessions where the red light creates a confessional atmosphere. Explore Dani's complex attraction—her usual assertiveness complicated by her desire to protect Elliot's vulnerability, her excitement at witnessing his self-discovery, and her confusion about what this means for her own identity as someone typically attracted to more defined gender presentations. The narrative should capture the tender awkwardness of their conversations, Dani's internal struggle between taking charge and wanting to give Elliot agency, and the unexpected chemistry that develops as both find themselves in uncharted territory. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Darkroom Revelations The safelight turns everything the color of wine. Of wounds. Of the inside of a closed eyelid. I've always loved that about the darkroom—the way it strips the world down to a single frequency and makes honesty feel less dangerous. Maybe that's why I suggested we book the 10 PM slot. Maybe that's why Elliot said yes without hesitating. He's standing at the developing station now, silver tongs hovering over the tray, watching a ghost image surface on the paper. Some shot he took of the courtyard through chain-link fence, all geometry and longing. He hums while he waits—something low and tuneless that vibrates in his chest more than his throat. A habit I've catalogued over the semester the way you catalogue anything you find yourself thinking about too often. "You're staring," he says. Not accusatory. Just observational, the way he says most things. "Professional interest," I lie. "Your technique needs work." He laughs. It's a quiet sound, barely louder than the chemical drip. "My technique is fine. You just like hovering." Fair. I do like hovering. I like the way his rings clink against the metal tongs. I like the way his black t-shirt—some washed-out band logo I can't read in this light—hangs off his shoulders like it knows something I don't. I like the smudge of developer on his jaw where he touched his face without thinking. I've spent four months not examining any of this too closely. The print finishes surfacing. He lifts it with careful hands, and I watch water trail down his wrists, over the veins, catching red light. He has beautiful hands. I've thought about them wrapped around a camera, wrapped around a coffee cup, wrapped around— I stop. I stop because I'm twenty-five and I know what I like and what I like has always been clear-edged and certain. People who take up space. People who know exactly what they want and reach for it. I have always been the one who reaches. "Dani." He sets the print on the drying rack and turns to face me. The safelight catches his eyes, makes them look amber. "Can I tell you something weird?" My pulse does something stupid. "Weird is the whole point of this class." He smiles, but it's nervous. He pulls at one of his earlobe piercings—the third one, the little silver crescent moon—and I can tell he's deciding whether to commit to whatever he's about to say. "I've been thinking about something. About myself." He pauses. "About gender, maybe. I don't know." The darkroom hums. The ventilation fan. The chemicals. His breathing, slightly unsteady. "Okay," I say. I'm good at this. I'm good at being steady for people. "Tell me." He tells me. Haltingly, in fragments, the way a photograph surfaces—first the vague shapes, then the details sharpening. How he's never felt wrong, exactly, but never felt right either. How he tried on a skirt at a thrift store last month and felt something crack open in his chest. How he's been wearing eyeliner to class and no one's said anything, but he doesn't know if that's acceptance or invisibility. How he's not sure if he wants to be seen differently or just seen more clearly. How he doesn't know if there's a word for it yet, or if he needs one. He talks for ten minutes. I don't interrupt. This is the hardest thing I've ever done, and I once spent six hours in a critique where a professor told me my entire portfolio was "derivative nonsense." When he finishes, he looks terrified. Like he's handed me something breakable and is waiting to see if I'll fumble it. "Thank you for telling me," I say. Because I've learned enough to know that's the first right thing. "That's a lot to carry around." "It is," he agrees. "It's a lot." I want to say something brilliant. I want to say something that fixes it, that makes him feel found. I'm good at fixing. I'm good at plans and next steps. But I don't know the next step here, and something in me recognizes that maybe there isn't one yet. Maybe the next step is just this: standing in red light while someone shows you the unedited version of themselves. "I don't know what I am," he says. "That's the stupid part. I just know I'm not what everyone assumes." "That's not stupid. That's the whole point of art, isn't it? Seeing what's actually there instead of what you expected." He looks at me, and something shifts. I watch him decide to trust me, and it's the most intimate thing I've ever witnessed. "You're really good at this," he says. "At listening. You seem like you'd be bad at it." I laugh, surprised. "Thanks?" "No, I mean—" He flushes, or I think he does. Hard to tell in red light. "You're so... you. All the time. You walk into a room and you know exactly who you are. I've been jealous of that all semester." The ache that blooms in my chest is unfamiliar. I want to tell him I don't know who I am either, not really, not underneath the confidence I've built like armor. I want to tell him that watching him figure himself out is the bravest thing I've seen, braver than anything I've done by being loud. I want to tell him that my certainty about what I'm attracted to is developing cracks, and I don't know if that's terrifying or wonderful. I say none of this. Instead, I pick up a fresh sheet of photo paper and slide it across the counter to him. "Show me," I say. "Whatever you're figuring out. Show me through the work." He takes the paper. His fingers brush mine, and we both pretend not to notice. He starts humming again—that low, tuneless vibration—and I lean against the wall and watch him work, and I think about how the darkroom is the only place where you can watch something become itself in complete darkness. How you have to trust the process. How you have to wait. I'm good at a lot of things. I'm learning that waiting might not be one of them. But for him, I'm trying.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical craft and emotional intelligence in handling a delicate subject matter. The narrative successfully captures Dani's voice as an assertive art student while showing her vulnerability when confronted with unfamiliar territory. The darkroom setting is expertly utilized both literally and metaphorically, creating an intimate confessional atmosphere that serves the story's themes of revelation and development. The characterization is particularly strong. Dani's internal conflict between her usual take-charge nature and her desire to give Elliot agency feels authentic and well-developed. Her attraction is portrayed with complexity - not just physical but intellectual and emotional, complicated by her own questioning of what this means for her identity. Elliot emerges as a fully realized character despite limited dialogue, with his vulnerability and self-discovery rendered believably. The prose quality is consistently high, with evocative imagery that serves the story rather than overwhelming it. The opening lines about the safelight's color establish mood effectively, and the photography metaphors are woven naturally throughout. The writing demonstrates restraint - the author resists over-explaining or rushing toward resolution, instead allowing the moment to breathe and develop organically. The dialogue feels natural and serves character development well. Elliot's halting revelation about gender identity is handled with sensitivity and authenticity. Dani's responses show her growth in real-time as she learns to listen rather than direct. The piece adheres closely to the prompt requirements, capturing the specific dynamic requested between these characters in this setting. The ending avoids false resolution while providing emotional satisfaction through Dani's recognition of her own growth and willingness to be patient. Minor weaknesses include some moments where the internal monologue could be tightened, and occasionally the metaphorical language borders on being slightly overwrought, though it generally stays within appropriate bounds for the literary style. + Contemporary romance / drama: Stolen Moments in Roma — Score: 80.0
Prompt:
Contemporary Romance / Drama: Stolen Moments in Roma. Write a series of interconnected short scenes and dialogues that span a single day in modern-day Rome, in narrative form. This will form the beginning of a larger story. Alessandra, a fiery, independent Roman woman who works as a motorcycle courier, nearly collides with Ben, a somewhat clumsy, overly polite British academic on sabbatical, who is engrossed in a guidebook near the Trevi Fountain. The initial encounter is a clash of cultures and personalities – Alessandra is all sharp wit and rapid-fire Italian, while Ben is flustered apologies and awkward attempts at speaking the language. However, as they sort out the near-miss, a spark of unexpected humor and connection emerges. Alessandra, it quickly becomes clear, is a whirlwind of energy and mischief. She's a self-admitted troublemaker with a secret penchant for kleptomania, a detail she doesn't explicitly reveal, but which manifests in subtle, daring acts throughout the day. She impulsively decides to take Ben on a wild, unsanctioned tour of Rome, dragging him in some specific instances far outside his comfort zone. Ben, despite his initial apprehension and inherent politeness, finds himself utterly captivated by Alessandra's unapologetic spirit and zest for life. He is drawn into her orbit, a willing participant in increasingly outlandish situations, a quiet rebellion brewing beneath his scholarly exterior. The narrative should primarily consist of short, vivid scenes and dialogue exchanges, revealing their contrasting personalities and backgrounds, and the growing, ambiguous tension between them. Ben is captivated by Alessandra's energy and the thrill of breaking free from his routine. There's a clear sense that Alessandra is toying with Ben, pushing his boundaries and enjoying his reactions, but her own feelings remain somewhat enigmatic. The day should be a rollercoaster of exhilarating, slightly dangerous, and humorous moments, leaving Ben completely disoriented but utterly smitten. Explore the underlying tension & chemistry between them, culminating in some payoff as they part ways (or not -- your choice). Include this detail: Write in narrative prose, using immersive storytelling. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Stolen Moments in Roma ## I. Near the Fountain, 9:47 AM The guidebook said the Trevi Fountain was best experienced in morning light, when the travertine glowed like warm bone and the crowds hadn't yet thickened into an impenetrable human paste. What the guidebook did not mention—and Ben would later consider writing to the publisher about this—was the possibility of being killed beside it. The Vespa came from nowhere. Or rather, it came from a narrow gap between a delivery van and a tourist bus that no sane vehicle should have attempted, threading the needle at a speed that suggested its rider either had a death wish or complete faith in divine intervention. Ben stepped backward, tripped over his own heel, and sat down hard on the fountain's edge as the scooter slewed to a stop close enough to ruffle the pages of his open book. The rider pulled off her helmet. Dark hair tumbled out in a deliberately cinematic way that Ben suspected was practiced. She was striking—not beautiful in the soft, guidebook-illustration sense, but sharp-featured, watchful, like a hawk that had learned to operate heavy machinery. "*Ma sei scemo?*" she demanded. "You stand in the middle of the street reading a book? In Roma? We invented traffic! We *perfected* it!" "I—that is—*mi dispiace?*" Ben managed, his Italian shaped by six months of evening classes and a pronunciation guide that had clearly lied to him. She stared at him. Then she laughed—a short, startled sound, as if she hadn't expected it from herself. "Your accent," she said, switching to English with theatrical disdain. "It is like a cat being neutered." "I—beg your pardon?" "What are you reading?" She snatched the guidebook before he could respond, flipping through it with the casual contempt of someone examining a menu in a bad restaurant. "Ah. *Fodor's*. You came to Roma with *Fodor's*." "It came highly recommended—" "By whom? Your grandmother?" She tucked the book into the inside pocket of her leather jacket in one fluid motion. "I am keeping this. For your safety. Come—we get coffee." "I don't actually—" "Coffee. Now. You almost died. In Italy, this requires coffee." ## II. Bar Sant'Eustachio, 10:20 AM She ordered for him without asking—a dense, almost violently strong espresso that made his eyes water and his pulse quicken in a way that felt medical. "Alessandra," she said, offering her hand across the tiny marble table. "I deliver packages. I make my own hours. I answer to no one." "Ben. I'm on sabbatical. From—" "Let me guess." She squinted at him. "You are a professor of something very boring. Literature? No—history. Medieval history. You wear your bag across your body like a woman afraid of pickpockets. You have a sunburn on only the back of your neck because you walk looking down. And you have not once looked at my chest, which means you are either very polite or very English." "Both, I should think." "Both," she repeated, smiling. "Yes. I thought so." ## III. Trastevere, 11:45 AM She drove like the city was a video game she'd already beaten. Ben rode behind her, arms locked around her waist, convinced he would never feel calm again. They stopped in a narrow street where laundry hung between buildings like faded flags of surrender. "This is where the real Roma lives," she said. "Not your Fodor's Roma. This one has teeth." She led him through a market, past stalls of bruised fruit and cheap leather, and he watched her fingers move—quick, light, almost unconscious. A silk scarf materialized in her jacket. A small ceramic figure joined it. She caught him watching and held his gaze with such direct, amused challenge that he felt the accusation die in his throat. "You see something?" she asked. "I—no. Nothing." "Good answer." She popped an unpurchased grape into her mouth. "Come. I show you something your book does not know about." ## IV. A Hidden Courtyard, 12:30 PM Through a door he would have walked past a thousand times, down a passage that smelled of damp stone and jasmine, into a courtyard where a fountain trickled moss-green and ancient. She sat on the edge, and he sat beside her, and for a moment the city's roar became distant, muffled, as if they'd stepped outside time. "Why are you doing this?" he asked. "Showing me all this?" She considered him. "Because you looked lost. And because I was bored. And because—" She stopped, tilting her head. "You have a nice neck. For an academic." "I don't know what to do with that information." "You could blush. You're very good at it." ## V. Near the Pantheon, 2:15 PM The man appeared from a shop doorway—older, thick-necked, with the watchful eyes of someone who'd seen too much. "*Alessandra.* Still alive, then. Still making trouble." "*Marco.*" Her voice carried a complicated history. "Still selling overpriced leather to Germans?" Marco turned to Ben with the weary concern of a man delivering necessary bad news. "You are with her? *Amico*—listen. This one, she is... how do you say. A hurricane in a nice dress. She will take everything you have and you will thank her for it." He leaned closer. "She once stole my dog." "It was a temporary borrowing," Alessandra said. "And he was neglected." "He was *not*—she still has the collar. I saw it in her apartment." "Sentimental value." She took Ben's arm. "We're leaving. *Ciao*, Marco." As they walked, Ben said, "Do you still have the collar?" She said nothing, but her smile was answer enough. ## VI. Ponte Sisto, Sunset They stood on the bridge as the sky turned the color of a wound—orange and deep red bleeding into purple. The Tiber moved below them, sluggish and old. "I should go," Ben said. "I've—I have things. Research. Notes to review." "Of course. Your sabbatical. Very important." "Alessandra—" "Ben." "Will I see you again?" She turned to face him fully. The dying light caught her features, sharpening them. She reached into her jacket and withdrew his guidebook—dog-eared now, with a phone number scrawled across the cover in red ink. "Page forty-seven," she said. "I made a correction. Your Fodor's is wrong about the church hours. Also page ninety-two. Also—" She paused, stepping closer. "Also, I may have written my number on several other pages. You'll have to find them." "That's—" "Annoying? Yes. I know." She kissed him—brief, firm, like a statement of intent—and pulled back before he could respond. "Find them all, *professore*. Then maybe I give you back your dignity." She was on the Vespa and gone before he could speak, weaving into traffic with the casual insanity of someone who belonged to the chaos. He stood on the bridge, holding a stolen guidebook that had somehow become a treasure map, and realized he was smiling. He'd been in Rome for two weeks. It was only now, he thought, that he'd actually arrived.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and creative flair in crafting a contemporary romance set in Rome. The author successfully creates two distinct, compelling characters whose personalities emerge clearly through dialogue and action rather than exposition. Alessandra's kleptomaniac tendencies are woven subtly throughout the narrative as requested, manifesting in quick, light-fingered moments that feel natural rather than forced. The structure works well, using numbered vignettes that create a cinematic quality while maintaining narrative flow. The pacing moves briskly through the day, building tension and chemistry effectively. The dialogue crackles with personality - Alessandra's sharp wit ("Your accent... it is like a cat being neutered") contrasts beautifully with Ben's flustered politeness, creating authentic cultural and personality clashes. The Rome setting is rendered with specificity and atmosphere. Rather than relying on tourist-brochure descriptions, the author creates a lived-in city with "teeth," using details like laundry hanging "like faded flags of surrender" and the Tiber moving "sluggish and old." The contrast between guidebook Rome and authentic Rome serves the thematic purpose well. Character development feels earned. Ben's transformation from nervous academic to someone willing to be swept into Alessandra's orbit happens gradually through the day's events. Alessandra remains appropriately enigmatic while revealing enough vulnerability (the dog collar detail) to suggest depth beyond her mischievous exterior. The ending strikes an appropriate note - romantic but not overly saccharine, with the phone number hidden in the guidebook providing a clever callback to the opening collision. The final line about "arriving" in Rome works both literally and metaphorically. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose edges toward the precious ("like a hawk that had learned to operate heavy machinery"), and a few dialogue exchanges that feel slightly constructed rather than completely natural. The kleptomania element, while handled well, could have been integrated even more seamlessly. [Scores] Adherence to Instructions: 18 Believable Character Actions: 17 Nuanced Characters: 16 Consistent Voice/Tone of Writing: 17 Imagery and Descriptive Quality: 16 Elegant Prose: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Emotionally Complex: 14 Coherent: 18 Meandering: 3 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 6 Overwrought: 5 Incongruent Ending Positivity: 3 Unearned Transformations: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Sentences Flow Naturally: 15 Overall Reader Engagement: 16 Overall Impression: 16

+ Cozy sci-fi: The Last Outpost — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Cozy sci-fi The Last Outpost Nettle (or Nets to her friends) bartends at The Last Outpost -- a lazy hub for miners and merchants in an asteroid belt on the Andromeda outer rim. Escaping from her past life, Nets found a home here at the Outpost. They have, thus far, tolerated Net's flaws which she tries to keep in equilibrium with her assortment of talents. Her evenings involve chatting with patrons, negotiating the drama, intrigue, problems & love lives of the diverse crowd that filter through the Outpost. Tonight is no different, at least, until a frazzled looking stranger, tentacled dreads askew, quietly plops down at the bar: "Vega whisky, neat. Better make that a double." Write a chapter in this story. Establish conflict for narrative interest. Don't resolve the conflict within this chapter. Aim for about 60% dialogue. First person, present tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Last Outpost ## Chapter One: The Quiet Type The thing about tending bar at the edge of nowhere is that everyone who sits down has a story, and about half of them want to tell it. The other half want you to pretend they don't exist. You learn to read which is which pretty quick. This one — tentacled dreads hanging limp and slightly askew, like they'd been grabbed and yanked recently — was a pretender. Shoulders hunched forward, eyes fixed on the bartop. Asking for whisky by name and specifying the pour. Someone who wanted the drink to do the talking. I grabbed the Vega. Good bottle. Three hundred years old, give or take, and it still tasted like peat and regret. I poured a double, neat, and set it down on a cork coaster. "Running a tab or closing out?" "Closing." A pause. "For now." I nodded and wiped the spot on the bar in front of me. Same spot I'd been wiping for the last ten minutes. The rag was doing its job, which was mostly giving my hands something to do while my ears stayed open. The stranger threw back half the whisky in one go. Winced. Set the glass down with trembling fingers. "Long haul?" I asked. "Excuse me?" "Your hands. Shaking like you've been on a burn-run. Long haul between gates messes with the inner ear, throws off the motor control." "Something like that." I kept wiping. The spot was very clean. Mira appeared at the far end of the bar, waving her empty glass. I held up a finger — *one minute* — and she nodded, slouching back against the wall to chat with old Gorsky. "You're Nettle," the stranger said. I stopped wiping. "Most people call me Nets." "I know. I came here looking for you." My hand found the baton I keep under the bar. Smooth metal, weighted at the end. A gift from a Korvath dockworker who said I'd need it someday. "That so?" "I'm not here to cause trouble." The stranger raised both hands, palms out. Tentacled dreads shifted, and I caught the glint of a neural port at the base of their skull — military grade, old model, the kind they stopped installing about fifteen years ago. "I need a place to hide. Someone told me you understand what that means." "Who told you that?" "Does it matter?" "It does to me." The stranger took another sip. Smaller this time. Savoring it. "A woman named Suki. Said you worked together, a long time ago." The baton handle got very warm in my grip. Suki. I hadn't heard that name in six years. Hadn't wanted to. "Suki's dead," I said. "That's what I thought too." The bar noise seemed to fade. Mira's laugh, Gorsky's coughing, the hum of the air recycler — all of it pulled back like a tide going out. "What exactly are you telling me?" I asked. "I'm telling you that three days ago, I was running a salvage op in the Drift, and I picked up a distress signal. Encoded. Military frequency. I cracked it because I'm stupid and curious, and the message inside was a name and a location." The stranger tapped the bar. "Your name. This location. And a voice I didn't recognize saying she needed to warn you." "Warn me about what?" "Didn't say. The signal cut out after forty seconds. But I traced the origin point, and it's coming from inside the belt. Close. Maybe two hours' burn from here." I resumed wiping. Same spot. Back and forth. The rag moved and my mind raced and nothing showed on my face because I'd gotten very good at that particular trick. "Who else knows about this signal?" "That's the problem." The stranger drained the rest of the whisky. "I wasn't the only one who picked it up. There's a guild cutter parked at the edge of the belt. They've been running sweeps for the last six hours. I slipped past them, but they're going to find this place eventually." "Guild doesn't have jurisdiction out here." "Since when does that stop them?" Fair point. "And you came anyway," I said. "Why? You could've ignored the signal. Sold the coordinates. Walked away." The stranger's dreads shifted again — embarrassment, maybe, or something like it. "Suki saved my life once. Back when she was still running with the Freehand. I owed her." "The Freehand hasn't existed in—" "I know when it existed. I know what happened to it. And I know what you did." Those eyes met mine for the first time. Steady now, despite the shaking hands. "I'm not here to judge, Nets. I'm here because someone asked for help, and I'm asking you to give it." The baton stayed where it was. I let go of it slowly. "Finish your drink," I said. "I need to make a call." "Who are you calling?" I picked up the comm unit, old and battered, bolted to the wall behind the bar. "Someone who knows more about military signals than either of us." The stranger nodded and turned back to the empty glass. I dialed. It rang four times. Then a voice — gruff, familiar, annoyed — crackled through the speaker. "This better be important. I'm sleeping." "Jax. It's Nets." A pause. The sound of rustling. "Nets, it's three in the morning." "I know. Listen to me carefully. I need you to pull up any guild cutter activity in the belt, right now, and tell me what you see." Another pause. Longer this time. When Jax spoke again, the annoyance was gone. "How bad?" "I don't know yet. But I think it's about to get worse." I glanced at the stranger, who was watching me with an expression I couldn't quite read. Hope, maybe. Or desperation. The spot on the bar gleamed under the lights. Still clean. Still there.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt requirements, delivering a cozy sci-fi bar scene with approximately 60% dialogue in first-person present tense. The author establishes Nettle as a competent bartender with a mysterious past, creating an authentic voice that feels lived-in and professional. The stranger's arrival and revelation about Suki creates genuine tension and conflict that remains unresolved, as requested. The characterization is particularly strong. Nettle's bartending expertise comes through naturally in her observations about patrons and her practiced movements. Her wariness and defensive instincts feel earned rather than forced. The stranger is well-crafted as someone clearly in distress but trying to maintain composure, with physical details like the trembling hands and askew tentacled dreads effectively conveying their state. The dialogue feels natural and serves multiple purposes - advancing plot, revealing character, and building tension. Conversations flow organically, with realistic pauses and subtext. The exchange about the whisky and Nettle's reading of the stranger's condition demonstrates her expertise while establishing rapport. The prose strikes an appropriate balance for the cozy sci-fi genre - descriptive enough to establish atmosphere without becoming overwrought. Details like the "peat and regret" whisky and the worn bar rag create texture without excess. The pacing builds tension effectively, moving from routine bar interaction to the revelation about Suki and the guild threat. The world-building feels authentic, with references to gates, burn-runs, neural ports, and the guild creating a believable future setting. The Outpost comes across as a genuine frontier establishment where people with complicated pasts might find refuge. However, some elements feel slightly predictable - the mysterious stranger seeking the bartender with a past is a familiar trope, though it's executed competently. The revelation structure follows expected beats, and while the writing is solid, it doesn't surprise or innovate significantly within its genre conventions. The emotional complexity is present but not deeply explored in this opening chapter, which is appropriate for establishing the scenario. The piece successfully creates intrigue about Nettle's past and the current threat without resolving anything prematurely. + Young adult drama: Gone South — Score: 83.7
Prompt:
Young adult drama Gone South Taylor is a 16 year old teen with a history of "concerning behavioural patterns" (or that's how the stupid smug school counsellor worded it on the phone call to Mom). It shouldn't be a surprise: When you live in a backwater town in Tennessee, your choices are: a. be mind-fuckingly bored, or b. invent some drama. Two days ago, Taylor came out to their family as trans. This disclosure was *not* in the category of invented drama -- it was, in fact, the real deal, and a long time coming. It didn't go well. The last 48 hours had been pure disaster. In a moment of desperate inspiration, Taylor had decided to get the hell out of dodge. And so an escape plan was hatched and executed within the space of 30 minutes. That was last night. Now Taylor was two states away, having been unceremoniously ejected from their hitched ride in downtown New Orleans. Write the next chapter in Taylor's story as they find their feet in this new environment. Tell the chapter over the course of their first day in New Orleans, in which Taylor meets another runaway with issues and secrets. Taylor navigates this new world, not knowing who to trust or what the dangers are. Write in Taylor's voice, and incorporate their internal monologue as they grapple with intense & conflicting emotions. Also incorporate dialogue with the interesting characters Taylor meets. First person, mixed tense. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Gone South ## Chapter Two: Crescent The strap on my backpack keeps slipping off my shoulder. It's doing it on purpose. Everything I own is in this bag and it's trying to escape, just like me. One change of clothes. A phone charger. Forty-three dollars and some change I stole from the jar on the kitchen counter that Mom calls "the vacation fund" even though nobody in our family has ever been on vacation. The air here is wrong. Thick. Wet. Like breathing through a hot dishcloth. It's six in the morning and already eighty degrees and I'm standing on a sidewalk on a street called Tchoupitoulas, which I cannot pronounce, and a man is peeing against a wall like it's a perfectly normal Tuesday activity, and maybe here it is. I walked four blocks before I found a gas station with a bathroom. The mirror was cracked and someone had written DIE SCUM across it in Sharpie, which, honestly, mood. I looked at myself — at the face I'm still learning to call mine — and thought: *Okay. You're here. Now what?* I don't know what. I bought a bottle of water and a pack of off-brand Pop-Tarts and the guy behind the counter didn't even look at me, which was great, because looking at people means questions and questions mean answers and answers mean *why are you alone in New Orleans at sixteen, and where are your parents, and what's your real name?* My real name is Taylor. That's the one thing I'm sure of. --- By ten I've found the river. There's a park, a strip of green with a walkway, and I sit on a bench and eat my Pop-Tarts and watch a cargo ship slide past like a slow metal dinosaur. The Mississippi is brown. I don't know what I expected. Blue, maybe. Everything about this place is different from home and I keep cataloguing the differences like I'm studying for a test. *No hills. No smell of cow shit. No one who knows my deadname.* That last one matters. "You eating those or just staring at them?" I look up. A girl, maybe seventeen, is standing a few feet away. She's got short hair, buzzed on one side, and she's wearing a denim jacket with patches sewn all over it. A faded backpack hangs off one shoulder. She looks like she's been sleeping outside for a while but she's also got this energy, this *alertness*, like a stray cat who's figured out which humans throw food and which ones throw rocks. "Eating them," I say. "Slowly." "Smart. Make them last." She sits down on the other end of the bench without asking. "I'm Maren." "Taylor." "You just get here?" I consider lying. I consider saying I'm visiting family, I'm waiting for my mom, I'm a normal kid doing normal kid things. But there's something about Maren — maybe the way she's sitting, one knee pulled up to her chest, like she's ready to bolt — that makes lying feel stupid. "Last night," I say. "Greyhound?" "Hitched." She looks at me. Really looks. "That was dumb." "Yeah." "Like, *really* dumb." "I know." "Okay." She nods. "Just checking that you know." --- Maren has been in New Orleans for three months. She tells me this as we walk through the French Quarter, past buildings with iron balconies and music already leaking out of doorways at eleven in the morning. She tells me about the shelters, the ones that are okay and the ones where you don't go unless you want your stuff stolen. She tells me which churches hand out food on which days. She tells me about a coffee shop on Decatur where the owner lets kids sit for hours if they buy one drink. "You're just... telling me all this?" I say. "You don't even know me." "I know you just got here. I know you hitchhiked, which means you're either desperate or stupid, and you don't seem stupid. And I know—" She stops. Looks at me sideways. "I know what it's like to be somewhere new with nothing and no one." I want to ask her story. I don't. There's a closed door behind her eyes and I recognize it because I've got one too. "Where are you sleeping tonight?" she asks. "Working on it." "There's a spot. Under the expressway, near the train tracks. A bunch of us stay there. It's not safe-safe, but it's safer than the park." *Us.* I notice the word. The way it implies a community, a *we*. I haven't been part of a *we* in a long time. Maybe ever. "Okay," I say. "Maybe." --- By afternoon the heat is a physical weight. We're sitting on the levee, me and Maren, and she's rolling a cigarette with tobacco she pulled from a pouch in her bag. She offers me one. I shake my head. "Good," she says. "Don't start." She lights hers and squints at the river. For a moment she looks older than seventeen. She looks ancient, actually. Like someone who's seen too much and compressed it all into a few years. "So what's your deal?" she asks. "If you want to share. If not, whatever." The question sits between us. I could pick it up or leave it. My chest feels tight. Two days ago I told my parents I was trans and my father said *not in my house* and my mother cried like I'd told her I was dying, and the thing is, I *was* dying, just not the way she meant. I was dying in that house. Slowly. Like something left in a jar without air. "I'm trans," I say. Out loud. To a stranger on a levee in Louisiana. And my voice doesn't shake, which surprises me. Maren takes a drag of her cigarette. "Cool," she says. Like I've told her my shoe size. And somehow that casualness, that total lack of drama, makes my eyes burn. "My parents didn't take it well," I add. "Yeah. Mine either." She pauses. "Not about the trans thing. About other things." She doesn't elaborate. I don't push. We sit there. The river keeps moving. My backpack strap slips again and I yank it back and Maren laughs, this short surprised sound, and I realize it's the first time I've heard her laugh. "You should safety-pin that," she says. "I don't have a safety pin." "I do." She digs through her bag. Pulls out a small bent pin. "Here. Everything's fixable if you've got the right tools." I take the pin. Our fingers brush. She's warm. *Everything's fixable.* I want to believe that. I pin the strap and it holds, and I think about my mother's face and my father's voice and the counsellor's stupid word — *concerning* — and I think about how I'm sixteen and alone in a strange city with forty-one dollars left and a girl I just met who carries safety pins in her bag like she's prepared for the world to fall apart at any moment. Maybe she is. Maybe we both are. The sun is starting its long slow slide toward the rooftops and Maren stubs out her cigarette and stands up and brushes off her jeans. "Come on," she says. "I'll show you the coffee shop. You can meet some people." I stand. My backpack stays on my shoulder. It holds. "Okay," I say. And I follow her.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and emotional authenticity in depicting a transgender teen's first day as a runaway in New Orleans. The author successfully captures Taylor's voice through a mix of vulnerability, defiance, and observational wit that feels genuinely adolescent without being precious. The characterization is particularly strong. Taylor emerges as a complex protagonist - scared but determined, cynical yet hopeful. The internal monologue feels authentic, balancing teenage snark ("honestly, mood") with genuine emotional depth. Maren is well-crafted as a secondary character, avoiding the trap of being either too mysterious or too revealing. Her casual acceptance of Taylor's trans identity feels natural rather than performative. The setting work is excellent. New Orleans comes alive through specific, sensory details - the unpronounceable street name, the thick humid air, the brown Mississippi. The author avoids tourist-trap clichés while still capturing the city's distinctive atmosphere. The progression from gas station to river to French Quarter feels organic and purposeful. The dialogue is naturalistic and serves character development well. Conversations feel like actual teenagers talking, with appropriate pauses, deflections, and the kind of careful probing that happens between strangers who recognize kinship. The exchange about safety pins works both literally and metaphorically without being heavy-handed. Structurally, the piece follows Taylor's emotional and physical journey effectively. The pacing allows for both action and reflection. The ending strikes an appropriate balance - hopeful without being unrealistic, suggesting possibility without guaranteeing easy solutions. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the metaphors feel slightly forced (the backpack strap "doing it on purpose"), and a few instances where the prose edges toward being overly polished for a first-person teenage narrator. The piece also relies somewhat on familiar runaway narrative beats, though it executes them well. The emotional complexity is handled with skill. Taylor's conflicted feelings about family, identity, and survival are layered throughout without becoming overwrought. The piece earns its moments of connection and hope through the authentic development of the relationship with Maren. + Dystopic-comedy: My Apocalypse Buddy — Score: 78.8
Prompt:
My Apocalypse Buddy Write a wry, dry short story of survival and adventure loosely in the style of Terry Pratchett, set in the post-technological collapse world. The story begins with the pair deliberating their predicament and intent for the day's mission, details the ways in which the mission inevitably goes sideways, and returns them to home base in multiple ways, worse for wear. [characters] - the animo-tronic stuffed koala, Chewy, from some long forgotten before time - midas, our hapless protagonist, who has a knack for finding himself in the worst kinds of trouble. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# My Apocalypse Buddy The morning sun crept over the ruins of what had once been a shopping mall, casting long shadows across Midas's makeshift shelter—a repurposed freezer unit that had outlived both its manufacturer and, presumably, most of his customer base. "The way I see it," Midas said, addressing his companion, "we've got two options. We can stay here and definitely starve, or we can go to the old pharmacy and probably die." Chewy stared at him with those unblinking glass eyes that had seen the Before Times, his synthetic fur matted with the grime of countless adventures. The animatronic koala was strapped to Midas's pack, positioned so they could maintain what Midas generously called "conversation." "Choosy moms choose Jif," Chewy said, his speaker crackling with the ghost of commerce. "That's... yeah, I don't know what that means, buddy." Midas checked his makeshift weapon—a cricket bat with nails hammered through it, because nothing said 'civilization has ended' quite like improvised bludgeoning implements. "But I'm choosing to interpret that as enthusiasm. Let's move." --- The pharmacy was three blocks away, which in the post-collapse world translated to roughly six hours of careful navigation, territorial negotiations, and creative swearing. They made it two blocks before trouble found them, because trouble had a sort of homing beacon where Midas was concerned. This time, trouble wore the form of a feral dog pack—five mutts who'd gone wild and, from their expression, had developed a strong opinion about humans. "Midas," Chewy's voice box intoned, "I'm lovin' it." "Not. Helping." Midas backed against a rusted-out car, bat raised. The dogs circled, and he could see the calculation in their eyes. The calculation said: *this one looks stringy, but beggars can't be choosers.* He swung. The first dog yelped and retreated. The second was less easily convinced and caught his arm in its jaws. Midas screamed, slammed the beast against the car, and broke free. The pack regrouped, reassessing. "Maybe she's born with it," Chewy offered. "Maybe it's Maybelline." The dogs, perhaps unsettled by the koala's cryptic pronouncements, decided to seek easier prey. Midas slumped against the car, arm bleeding freely. "Thanks," he told Chewy. "Really turned the tide there." --- They reached the pharmacy at midday, which was fortunate because Midas needed antibiotics and unfortunate because the building was already occupied by a group of scavengers who had strong feelings about property rights. There were three of them, armed with proper weapons—guns, which were cheating as far as Midas was concerned. The apparent leader, a woman with a scar that split her face like a topographical map of poor decisions, leveled a rifle at his chest. "Wrong neighborhood, friend." "Look, I just need antibiotics. I'll take my supplies and go—" "You'll take nothing and go. If you go now, you go breathing." "What can brown do for you?" Chewy chimed in. The woman's eyes narrowed. "What's wrong with your koala?" "He's... he does that. Pre-collapse programming. Look, I'm leaving." Midas raised his hands, bat dangling uselessly. "No trouble." "Trouble found me anyway," he muttered twenty minutes later, sitting in an alley two blocks from the pharmacy, empty-handed, arm throbbing, pride wounded. A rat had also bitten his ankle during his retreat, which seemed unnecessary. --- The return journey took longer than the outbound, partly because Midas was bleeding from multiple locations and partly because he'd somehow wandered into the territory of a group that collected... he wasn't sure what. Bottlecaps? Memories? Human ears? He didn't wait to clarify. By the time he reached his shelter, the sun was setting, painting the ruins in colors that would have been beautiful if Midas wasn't fairly certain he was developing an infection. His arm had swollen to twice its normal size, his ankle had turned an interesting shade of purple, and someone had stolen his left shoe during a brief nap he'd taken behind a dumpster. "All in all," he told Chewy, settling into the freezer unit, "could have gone worse." "Can you hear me now?" Chewy asked. "Good." Midas considered this. The koala's random slogans had become a sort of oracle over their time together—nonsense that occasionally, in the right light, with enough desperation, almost made sense. "Yeah," Midas said, wrapping his arm with a strip of his shirt. "Yeah, I can hear you. Loud and clear, buddy." He closed his eyes. Tomorrow they'd try again. Tomorrow would be different. It wouldn't, of course. Tomorrow would be exactly like today—desperate, dangerous, and absurd. But Midas had learned that hope wasn't about expecting better outcomes. It was about getting up anyway, strapping your animatronic koala to your pack, and walking out into the ruins of a world that had ended but hadn't quite finished dying. "Zoom zoom zoom," Chewy said. "Mazda." Midas laughed until it hurt. It hurt quite a lot, actually.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece successfully captures the requested Terry Pratchett-inspired tone with dry wit and absurdist humor, particularly through the animatronic koala's random corporate slogans serving as inadvertent oracle pronouncements. The author demonstrates strong adherence to the prompt structure, following the mission planning, inevitable failure, and return home arc while maintaining the wry, observational voice throughout. The characterization works well within the constraints - Midas emerges as a believably hapless protagonist whose self-aware commentary on his situation creates genuine humor. The relationship dynamic between him and Chewy is cleverly handled, with the koala's pre-programmed advertising slogans creating both comedy and unexpected moments of meaning. The author shows restraint in not over-explaining this relationship. The prose maintains consistent quality with several standout descriptive moments: "casting long shadows across Midas's makeshift shelter—a repurposed freezer unit that had outlived both its manufacturer and, presumably, most of his customer base" demonstrates the kind of observational humor Pratchett was known for. The writing flows naturally and avoids purple prose while still creating vivid imagery. However, the piece suffers from some predictability in its mission structure - the dog attack and territorial scavengers feel somewhat routine for post-apocalyptic fiction. The emotional complexity, while present, remains surface-level, and some transitions between scenes feel slightly abrupt. The dialogue, while functional, doesn't reach the memorable quotability that characterizes the best Pratchett-inspired work. The ending strikes an appropriate balance between darkness and hope without feeling unearned, and the final philosophical reflection on hope feels genuine rather than forced. The piece maintains engagement throughout its length and successfully delivers on the prompt's requirements while creating an entertaining, coherent narrative. + Romance: Love in the Limelight — Score: 83.2
Prompt:
Romance: Love in the Limelight. Write one scene within a larger story set in Wales. A famous (fictional) actor ducks into a small-town bookstore to escape paparazzi. The scene takes us through the characters meeting in this odd circumstance. Over the course of the scene, the actor and the bookstore owner have a conversation charged by an undercurrent of unspoken chemistry. Write the actor as somewhat of a rogue with a fragile ego, which needs to be fed by having everyone like him. He is thoroughly charming, but the bookstore owner seems (at least superficially) immune to this; which paradoxically provokes a genuine attraction and derails the charm offensive. The bookstore owner, despite the superficial rebuffs of the actor's charm, is inwardly more than a little charmed and flustered despite themselves. Write primarily in dialogue, in the distinct voices of each character. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Last Chapter The bell above the door hadn't even finished its ring before he was pressing his back against the wood, one hand holding it shut. "Are you closed?" Ffion looked up from the till. The man was wearing a baseball cap pulled low and sunglasses despite the fact that it was raining sideways in Aberystwyth, which meant it was approximately eleven o'clock—morning or night, take your pick. "We're open." "Brilliant. Fantastic. I just need to—" He pressed his face to the window, peering down the street. "How long is this road?" "Forever, if you keep walking. Are you being followed, or are you just very thorough about checking for rain?" He turned. Removed the sunglasses. And there it was—the face that had been plastered across the side of a bus in Cardiff she'd walked past three times this week. Rhys Cadogan. Currently starring in something with swords, if the poster had been any indication. "Right. So. You know who I am." "You're blocking my door." He stepped away from it, smiling. Not the bus-poster smile—something more deliberate, warmer, as if he'd dialed it up specifically for her. "Rhys." "Ffion. Can I help you find something, or are you just hiding?" "Both, ideally." He glanced around the shop, taking in the cramped aisles, the ceiling-high shelves, the smell of old paper and carpet that hadn't been replaced since the seventies. "This is brilliant. This is—God, this is exactly the kind of place my mother would drag me into as a kid and I'd pretend to hate it." "Did you." "Yeah. I was a terrible child. She'd leave with a stack of books this high—" He held his hand at his chest. "—and I'd sulk the whole way home. Then read them all." "What kind?" "Natural history, mostly. She was mad about it. Birds, specifically. Knew every bird in Wales by its call." He paused, and something shifted behind his eyes. "I picked it up from her, actually. The birding. Not the—well. I still can't tell a chiffchaff from a willow warbler by sight, but by sound, absolutely." Ffion's hand stilled on the till. "You're having me on." "I am absolutely not. Nothing more attractive than a man who knows his warblers, right?" He grinned. "I couldn't say." "Couldn't, or won't?" "Would you like a coffee? I've got a kettle in the back and no customers, apparently, because you've scared them off." "Love one. Milk, no sugar." She disappeared through the beaded curtain. From the back room, she called: "There's a peregrine pair nesting on the cliff behind the old college. I've been watching them since March." The silence that followed was so sudden she thought he might have left. Then, quietly: "You're serious." "I'm always serious. It's a character flaw." "It's not a flaw." His voice had lost something—the performance of it. When she came back through the curtain with two mugs, he was standing by the natural history section, fingers trailing along the spines. He'd taken off the cap. "You've got a first edition of Lockley's *Island of Skomer*." "First edition, third printing. Don't get too excited." "Too late." He took the mug from her, and his fingers brushed hers. He held the contact a beat too long. "So. Peregrines. You've got a scope, or...?" "Scope and everything. Borrowed it from the wildlife trust. I take the Tuesday morning shift counting the pairs along the coast." "Counting them." "For the census. You can't just watch peregrines, Rhys. You have to *contribute*." He laughed—a real one, startled out of him. "I once spent four hours in a hide in the Brecon Beacons waiting for a goshawk and I told absolutely no one because I thought it made me sound—" "Interesting?" "I was going to say 'uncool.'" "Those aren't mutually exclusive." She sipped her coffee. "You missed a chiffchaff outside, by the way. About ten minutes before you came in." "You're killing me." "It was right by the door. The one you were blocking." He stared at her. The charm was still there, somewhere in the set of his shoulders, but it had gone ragged at the edges. He kept opening his mouth, closing it. "Can I come back?" he said finally. "Tuesday. For the count." "It's not a social event." "I know." "It's cold, and boring, and I'll tell you to be quiet at least six times." "I'm very good at being quiet." She raised an eyebrow. "I can be," he amended, quieter. Outside, a car passed slowly. He glanced at the window, then back at her, and in that look was something unguarded and almost desperate—the need to be seen as more than the face on the bus. "Tuesday," she said. "Seven sharp. Bring your own flask." "Seven sharp." He set the mug down on the counter, carefully, like it mattered that he didn't chip it. "Ffion." "Rhys." "Thank you for not making this weird." "I'm sure you'll manage that on your own." He laughed again—softer this time—and pulled the cap back on. The bell rang. The door closed. Through the window, she watched him walk down the street with his hands in his pockets, not looking back. She picked up his mug. Still warm. "Chiffchaff," she muttered to herself, pressing her cool fingers against the ceramic. "Honestly." From somewhere in the back of the shop, the kettle clicked off.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong craft in several key areas while maintaining the requested romantic tension and character dynamics. The author successfully creates two distinct voices - Rhys's performative charm that gradually becomes more genuine, and Ffion's dry, practical Welsh sensibility that masks her growing interest. The Welsh setting is well-established through place names (Aberystwyth, Cardiff, Brecon Beacons) and cultural details without feeling forced. The dialogue carries the scene effectively, with each character having a clear voice. Ffion's responses are consistently terse and practical ("Forever, if you keep walking"), while Rhys shifts from practiced charm to genuine vulnerability. The birding connection serves as an excellent vehicle for showing rather than telling their compatibility and his deeper character. The characterization adheres well to the prompt requirements. Rhys displays the fragile ego and need for approval, particularly in moments like "the need to be seen as more than the face on the bus." His charm offensive genuinely derails when faced with Ffion's immunity, leading to more authentic interaction. Ffion's internal conflict between surface resistance and genuine attraction is subtly conveyed through actions like her hand stilling on the till and her final moment with the warm mug. The pacing is well-controlled, building from the comedic setup of a celebrity hiding in a bookstore to genuine connection over shared interests. The ending feels earned rather than forced, with appropriate restraint that maintains romantic tension. Some minor weaknesses include occasional moments where the prose becomes slightly self-conscious, and a few exchanges that feel slightly constructed rather than entirely natural. The piece could have pushed the emotional complexity slightly deeper, though what's present is effective. The writing demonstrates professional-level craft with natural sentence flow, vivid but not overwrought imagery, and genuine emotional engagement. The piece successfully balances humor, romance, and character development within the constraints of a single scene. + Whalepunk fantasy: The Morska Conspiracy — Score: 77.0
Prompt:
Whalepunk fantasy The Morska Conspiracy The Morska thieves guild experiences a sudden power vacuum as its leader was arrested in a raid in the early hours of the morning. Aya, a young woman born into aristocracy, has a keen interest in both the sciences and the arcane, having inherited her mother's gift for immateria magick (an outlawed practice for which her mother was, regrettably, hanged by the neck). Aya, bored with the world of aristocracy, has been spending her nights moonlighting with this very guild (masked, naturally). Leveraging her latent ability to summon dark entities from the immateria, she quickly rose through the ranks of the guild. Now, recognising the opportunity of the sudden power vacuum, Aya and her close companions Katra and Simeon, make a play for heart of the guild's claim to power: an ancient scythan artifact, under guard of spell and key in the depths of the guild hq. In contrast with the desperate chaos they had navigated thus far, the entrance to the underhold felt suddenly too quiet. Aya braces herself and reaches for the door. Write the next scene as the trio enter the underhold and find themselves confronted by none other than the guild leader herself, decidedly *un*-incarcerated, and in possession of both weapons and bodyguards. Paint a vivid picture of of the confrontation. Story beats: Aya is captured and unmasked. The trio are captured. Aya attempts to stall their demise by negotiating on her value as a hostage -- as she grapples with the inevitable consequences of her role in the guild being brought to light. Incorporate dialogue in the authentic voices of the characters. Do not write a fight scene. Lean into the gritty Victorian aesthetic with the dialogue & worldbuilding, as well as elements of gothic horror. In this world, men trust brass and oil and steel; magicka is feared and barely understood. Write in third person past tense, incorporating both Aya's internal monologue, and a heavy dialogue component. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# The Morska Conspiracy The door swung inward on oiled iron hinges, and the silence poured out like something liquid. Aya had expected torchlight, the reek of stale ale, perhaps a drunk lieutenant slumped over the ledger desk. What she found instead was the warm amber glow of aether-lamps — expensive, steady, municipal-grade lighting that had no business burning in a thieves' underhold — and the woman herself. Vasha Morska sat behind the great ironwood desk as though she had never left it. As though the morning's arrest had been a minor inconvenience, an appointment she had attended and found dull. Her grey hair was pulled back severely, her coat still buttoned to the throat. On the desk before her lay a scatter of documents and, beside them, a loaded percussion revolver. Two enforcers flanked the chamber's rear entrance, holding short-barrelled shotguns with the mechanical patience of men paid by the week. "Ah," said Vasha. "The children." Aya's stomach turned to cold stone. Behind her, she heard Katra's breath catch — a small, sharp sound — and Simeon's pocket watch marking the silence like a metronome. *Tick. Tick. Tick.* "Shut the door, Simeon," Vasha said, without looking at him. "And do come forward. All of you. Slowly." They obeyed. There was nothing else to be done. The underhold had one entrance, and the two enforcers now blocked the only other exit. Aya's fingers tingled with the instinct to reach into the immateria — to pull something forth, something dark and shapeless that could fill this room with cold — but Vasha was watching her with the particular attention of a woman who had survived thirty years in the Morska by anticipating exactly that sort of desperation. "I confess I'm curious," Vasha continued, turning her revolver slightly on the desk, letting the lamplight crawl along its barrel. "Did you think me still in irons at Harrowgate? Or did you simply not think at all?" "We heard the raid went clean," Katra said. Her voice was steady enough, though Aya could see her hands trembling at her sides. "Everyone heard it." "Everyone heard what I *allowed* them to hear." Vasha's smile was thin and without warmth. "A useful fiction. I needed to see who, precisely, would scurry for the scraps the moment they believed the table empty." Her gaze settled on Aya's mask — a simple thing, black silk and wire, the kind any guild runner might wear. "And you've brought me something *quite* unexpected." The enforcers moved. They were efficient about it, professional, taking Simeon's arms first because Simeon was the largest, then Katra, who spat something foul and was cuffed for her trouble. Aya did not resist when the third man — she had not noticed a third, behind the door, *stupid, stupid* — took her wrists and bound them with leather cord. Then Vasha rose, and crossed the room, and lifted the mask from Aya's face. The silence that followed was of a different quality. Heavier. Aya watched the recognition move through Vasha's expression like weather across a moor — confusion first, then disbelief, then something dark and calculating that settled into the lines around her mouth. "Well," Vasha said softly. She did not step back. She studied Aya's face the way a naturalist might study a specimen pinned under glass. "Lady Aya Vethrundt. Of the *Vethrundt* estate. Daughter of Edric, who sits on the Governor's Council." Her voice dropped. "And daughter of Maren Vethrundt, who was hanged at Crossmarket for the practice of immateria conjuration some... fourteen years ago, was it?" Aya said nothing. Her heart was beating so hard she could feel it in her teeth. *Tick. Tick. Tick.* The watch. Simeon's watch. She wanted to scream at it to stop. "You've been *very* careful," Vasha continued, turning the mask over in her hands as though it might yield further secrets. "I'll grant you that. The guild has had aristocratic sympathisers before — funders, mostly, cowards who pay from a safe distance. But you... you've been in the thick of it. Hands dirty. Climbing the ranks." She tilted her head. "Why?" "Because the aristocracy is *boring*," Aya said, and was surprised to find her voice did not shake. "Because the world of brass and oil and etiquette is a cage, and I preferred the company of people who at least have the honesty to call themselves thieves." Vasha laughed — a genuine sound, or nearly so. "Oh, you *are* your mother's daughter. She had that same self-righteous theatre about her, if the old records are to be believed." The laughter faded. "Which makes you a *profoundly* valuable problem, girl. The guild cannot simply dispose of a Vethrundt. Your father would burn the city looking for whoever stained his name with a daughter's corpse. But I cannot *release* you, either, because you now know the underhold's layout, the vault's location, and — I suspect — a great deal more about our operations than any masked initiate should." "Then don't dispose of me," Aya said. The words came quickly now, tumbling ahead of her fear. "Keep me. My family would pay a ransom that could fund this guild for a decade. The Vethrundt estate holds shipping contracts across three ports — my father would mortgage every vessel he owns before he'd let a scandal like this reach the papers. A daughter, *thieving*. A daughter consorting with criminals." She met Vasha's eyes. "He'd pay anything to keep it quiet." "And if he refuses?" "He won't. Scandal is the one currency aristocrats fear more than poverty." Vasha was quiet for a long moment. The pocket watch ticked. Somewhere in the depths of the building, pipes groaned with the pressure of the harbour tide. "And your companions?" Vasha asked. "What am I to do with *them*?" "That depends entirely," Aya said carefully, "on how useful you find me." Vasha studied her a moment longer, then turned to her enforcers. "Put them in the hold cells. *Separately.*" *Tick.* The last sound Aya heard, before the door closed behind her, was Simeon's watch — still counting the seconds, faithful and mechanical, as though anything made of brass and steel could be trusted in a world like this.
Judge Evaluation:
[Analysis] This piece demonstrates strong technical execution and atmospheric worldbuilding within the whalepunk fantasy setting. The author successfully creates a tense confrontation scene that adheres closely to the prompt's requirements, incorporating all requested story beats while maintaining the specified Victorian gothic aesthetic. The prose is generally well-crafted, with effective use of sensory details and atmospheric elements. The opening line "The door swung inward on oiled iron hinges, and the silence poured out like something liquid" establishes mood effectively. The author maintains consistent third-person past tense throughout and balances Aya's internal thoughts with dialogue-driven action. Character voices feel authentic to the setting, particularly Vasha's calculated menace and Aya's aristocratic defiance. The dialogue serves multiple purposes - revealing character, advancing plot, and building tension. Vasha's lines like "Did you think me still in irons at Harrowgate? Or did you simply not think at all?" effectively establish her as a formidable antagonist while revealing the protagonists' miscalculation. The worldbuilding elements integrate naturally - aether-lamps, percussion revolvers, and the contrast between mechanical technology and feared magicka. The author successfully avoids writing a fight scene as requested, instead focusing on psychological tension and negotiation. However, the piece has some weaknesses. The revelation and subsequent negotiation feel somewhat predictable - the aristocrat-turned-thief using her status as leverage is not particularly surprising. Some descriptions border on overwrought, particularly the repeated emphasis on the pocket watch's ticking, which becomes heavy-handed symbolism. The emotional complexity could be deeper. While Aya shows some internal conflict about her dual identity, the piece doesn't fully explore the psychological weight of her situation or the betrayal of her companions' trust. The ending feels somewhat abrupt, cutting off just as the real consequences begin. The pacing is generally strong, building tension effectively through the confrontation, though some moments feel slightly rushed, particularly the transition from capture to negotiation.
